questions.question: Which article provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 of the Constitution establishes the Supreme Court of India.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Review' in India means:
options: The judiciary can declare laws unconstitutional
questions.explanation: Judicial Review gives the Supreme Court and High Courts the power to examine legislation and executive actions and declare them void if they violate the Constitution.

questions.question: The principle of 'Judicial Review' allows courts to:
options: Examine and strike down laws/executive actions that violate the Constitution
questions.explanation: Judicial Review (borrowed from the USA) allows Indian courts (especially SC and HCs) to examine the constitutional validity of legislation and executive actions, and strike them down if they violate the Constitution. Articles 13, 32, 226 provide for this.

questions.question: The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is laid down in:
questions.explanation: Article 131: Original jurisdiction of SC — disputes between states, or between Centre and states. Article 132-134: Appellate jurisdiction. Article 136: Special Leave Petition (SLP).

questions.question: The Collegium system for appointment of judges was established in:
questions.explanation: The Collegium system emerged from three Supreme Court cases: First Judges Case (1982), Second Judges Case (1993) — established collegium, Third Judges Case (1998) — clarified composition.

questions.question: Which Union Territory has its own Legislature and High Court?
questions.explanation: Delhi (National Capital Territory) and Puducherry are the only UTs with their own Legislative Assemblies (and thus elected governments). J&K is now a UT with Legislature after 2019.

questions.question: The Supreme Court of India was established on:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India was established on 28 January 1950, two days after the Constitution came into force (26 January 1950). It replaced the Federal Court of India.

questions.question: The original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court (Art 131) covers disputes between:
options: Government of India and one or more States
questions.explanation: Article 131 gives the SC exclusive original jurisdiction in disputes between: (a) GoI and one or more States; (b) GoI and States on one side and States on other; (c) Two or more States.

questions.question: 'Curative Petition' in the Supreme Court was recognized in which case?
options: Rupa Ashok Hurra v. Ashok Hurra (2002)
questions.explanation: The concept of a Curative Petition was established in Rupa Ashok Hurra v. Ashok Hurra (2002). It is the last constitutional remedy after the SC dismisses a review petition.

questions.question: High Court judges retire at the age of:
questions.explanation: High Court judges (Art 217) retire at 62 years of age. Supreme Court judges (Art 124) retire at 65 years. This is different from the 7th Pay Commission recommendation of 65 for HCs.

questions.question: The ADM Jabalpur case (1976) was overruled by the Supreme Court in:
questions.explanation: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla (1976) — where the SC infamously held that right to life can be suspended during Emergency — was explicitly overruled by the 9-judge bench in K.S. Puttaswamy (2017).

questions.question: 'NOTA' (None of the Above) option was introduced in Indian elections in:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court in PUCL v. Union of India (2013) directed the Election Commission to provide NOTA option. NOTA was first used in 5 State Assembly elections in November 2013.

questions.question: The Special Leave Petition (SLP) in the Supreme Court is filed under:
questions.explanation: Article 136 empowers the Supreme Court to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment or order of any court or tribunal (except military tribunals). This is a discretionary power.

questions.question: How many High Courts are there in India currently?
questions.explanation: India currently has 25 High Courts. The latest ones established include the High Court for Telangana (2019) and High Courts for Jammu, Srinagar (after J&K reorganization). Some HCs have benches in multiple cities.

questions.question: The concept of 'Judicial Activism' in India refers to:
options: Courts actively using their power to protect rights and check government excesses
Courts acting faster in disposing cases
questions.explanation: Judicial Activism refers to the tendency of judges to actively protect citizens' rights through expansive interpretations of constitutional provisions, PILs, and suo motu actions — going beyond traditional passive adjudication.

questions.question: India's Constitution is described as 'federal in form but unitary in spirit' because:
options: It has a strong Centre with residual powers and emergency provisions to override States
questions.explanation: India's Constitution has federal features (division of powers, bicameral legislature, independent judiciary) but unitary features dominate: single citizenship, integrated judiciary, strong Centre, emergency provisions that can override States.

questions.question: Which article deals with the original jurisdiction of the Supreme Court in disputes between the Centre and States?
questions.explanation: Article 131 confers original jurisdiction on the Supreme Court in disputes between the Government of India and one or more States, or between two or more States.

questions.question: The 'Basic Structure' doctrine was evolved by the Supreme Court in:
questions.explanation: The Basic Structure doctrine was established in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973) — a 13-judge bench decision by a 7:6 majority.

questions.question: The concept of 'Constitutional Morality' was highlighted in which recent Supreme Court case?
options: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018)
Joseph Shine v. Union of India (2018)
questions.explanation: Both Navtej Singh Johar (decriminalising Section 377) and Joseph Shine (striking down adultery law) relied heavily on Constitutional Morality — adherence to constitutional values, not social morality.

questions.question: The 'Collegium System' for appointment of judges is based on which landmark judgements?
options: First Judges Case (1981), Second Judges Case (1993), Third Judges Case (1998)
ADM Jabalpur (1976), Maneka Gandhi (1978), Minerva Mills (1980)
questions.explanation: The collegium system evolved through: First Judges Case (1981) — executive primacy; Second Judges Case (1993) — CJI's opinion has primacy; Third Judges Case (1998) — CJI to consult 4 senior judges.

questions.question: The retirement age of a Supreme Court judge is:
questions.explanation: Supreme Court judges retire at 65 years (Article 124). High Court judges retire at 62 years. This was the only age limit originally — fixed at 65 by the Constitution.

questions.question: 'Public Interest Litigation' (PIL) was developed by:
questions.explanation: PIL was developed by the Supreme Court of India in the 1980s, especially through Justices V.R. Krishna Iyer and P.N. Bhagwati, allowing any citizen to approach courts for public interest.

questions.question: Who is the custodian of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is the custodian and final interpreter of the Constitution. It has the power of judicial review to strike down any law or executive action that violates the Constitution.

questions.question: The tenure of the Chief Election Commissioner is:
options: 6 years or till age 65, whichever is earlier
questions.explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner holds office for 6 years or until age 65, whichever is earlier. The same applies to Election Commissioners. They can be removed only like a Supreme Court judge.

questions.question: The Indra Sawhney case (1992) ruled that total reservation cannot exceed:
questions.explanation: In Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (Mandal case, 1992), the Supreme Court upheld OBC reservation but set the ceiling at 50% for all reservations combined (with exceptions for extraordinary situations).

questions.question: NOTA (None of the Above) option on EVMs was introduced in:
questions.explanation: NOTA was introduced following a Supreme Court order (PUCL case, 2013). Voters can press the NOTA button to reject all candidates while exercising their right to vote.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court of India has the power to review its own judgments under Article 137. Statement 2: The power of judicial review is implicit in Article 32 and Article 226 of the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 137 gives the Supreme Court the power to review its judgments and Articles 32 and 226 imply judicial review.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Chief Justice of India is appointed by the President without any consultation. Statement 2: The Second Judges Case (1993) established the Collegium system for appointing judges to the higher judiciary.
questions.explanation: Only statement 2 is correct as the Second Judges Case did establish the Collegium system.

questions.question: Statement 1: Article 124(2) of the Constitution lays down the eligibility criteria for a Supreme Court judge. Statement 2: A judge of the Supreme Court can be removed from office through impeachment under Article 124(4).
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 124(2) sets eligibility criteria and Article 124(4) provides for removal through impeachment.

questions.question: Statement 1: The concept of 'Basic Structure' was introduced in the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973). Statement 2: The judgment in the Minerva Mills case (1980) reaffirmed the doctrine of 'Basic Structure'.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Kesavananda Bharati case introduced the concept and the Minerva Mills case reaffirmed it.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in disputes between the Centre and one or more states under Article 131. Statement 2: The Advisory Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is provided under Article 143.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 131 provides for original jurisdiction and Article 143 for advisory jurisdiction.

questions.question: Statement 1: The power to increase the number of judges in the Supreme Court is vested in the Parliament. Statement 2: The Constitution (Forty-second Amendment) Act, 1976, curtailed the judicial review power of the Supreme Court.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Parliament can increase the number of judges and the 42nd Amendment curtailed judicial review.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court can declare a law as unconstitutional if it violates the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution. Statement 2: The 'Basic Structure' doctrine was first applied in the Golaknath case (1967).
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct as the 'Basic Structure' doctrine was introduced later in Kesavananda Bharati case.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India has the power to appoint ad-hoc judges to the Supreme Court under Article 127. Statement 2: The Collegium for appointment of Supreme Court judges includes the Chief Justice and four senior-most judges.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 127 allows for ad-hoc judges and the Collegium includes the Chief Justice and four senior-most judges.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power of judicial review over the decisions of administrative authorities. Statement 2: The power of judicial review is limited to reviewing laws and not executive actions.
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct as judicial review extends to both laws and executive actions.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Constitution (Ninety-ninth Amendment) Act, 2014, established the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC). Statement 2: The NJAC was struck down by the Supreme Court in the Fourth Judges Case (2015).
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the 99th Amendment established NJAC and it was struck down in the Fourth Judges Case.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court of India has the power to review its own judgments under Article 137. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also review the judgments of the High Courts under Article 137.
questions.explanation: Article 137 grants the Supreme Court the power to review its own judgments.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Collegium system for appointing judges to the Supreme Court and High Courts was established by the Second Judges Case. Statement 2: The Collegium system has been criticized for lacking transparency and accountability.
questions.explanation: The Collegium system was established by the Second Judges Case and has been criticized for its shortcomings.

questions.question: Statement 1: The power of judicial review is an implied power under the Constitution of India. Statement 2: The power of judicial review is explicitly granted to the Supreme Court under Article 32.
questions.explanation: The power of judicial review is an implied power under the Constitution of India.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to declare a law passed by Parliament as unconstitutional under Article 13. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also strike down a law passed by a state legislature under Article 13.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has the power to declare laws as unconstitutional under Article 13.

questions.question: Statement 1: The concept of 'basic structure' of the Constitution was introduced by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case. Statement 2: The 'basic structure' doctrine limits the power of Parliament to amend the Constitution.
questions.explanation: The 'basic structure' doctrine was introduced in the Kesavananda Bharati case and limits Parliament's power.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to appoint ad-hoc judges under Article 127. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also appoint acting judges under Article 126.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has the power to appoint ad-hoc judges under Article 127.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in cases involving disputes between states under Article 131. Statement 2: The Supreme Court also has original jurisdiction in cases involving the enforcement of fundamental rights under Article 32.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in cases involving disputes between states and enforcement of fundamental rights.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to transfer cases from one High Court to another under Article 139. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also transfer cases from one subordinate court to another under Article 139.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has the power to transfer cases under Article 139.

questions.question: Statement 1: The National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) was established by the 99th Amendment Act. Statement 2: The NJAC was struck down by the Supreme Court as unconstitutional.
questions.explanation: The NJAC was established by the 99th Amendment Act and struck down by the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to review the decisions of administrative tribunals under Article 136. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also review the decisions of statutory authorities under Article 136.
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court has the power to review decisions under Article 136.

questions.question: Statement 1: The collegium system for appointing Supreme Court judges was introduced by the Second Judges Case. Statement 2: The First Judges Case held that the President's opinion is not binding on the appointment of judges.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Second Judges Case introduced the collegium and the First Judges Case dealt with the President's role.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court can issue writs under Article 32 for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights. Statement 2: High Courts can also issue writs under Article 226 for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Articles 32 and 226 empower the Supreme Court and High Courts respectively to issue writs.

questions.question: Statement 1: The doctrine of basic structure was introduced in the Kesavananda Bharati case. Statement 2: The Golaknath case held that Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Kesavananda Bharati case introduced the basic structure doctrine and the Golaknath case dealt with amendment powers.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in disputes between the Centre and states under Article 131. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has appellate jurisdiction in cases involving the interpretation of the Constitution under Article 132.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Articles 131 and 132 provide for original and appellate jurisdiction respectively.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Constitution (42nd Amendment) Act, 1976, added the words 'socialist' and 'secular' to the Preamble. Statement 2: The same amendment also introduced the concept of judicial review.
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct as the 42nd Amendment added 'socialist' and 'secular' but did not introduce judicial review.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court can advise the President on matters of public importance under Article 143. Statement 2: The President can seek the opinion of the Supreme Court on any question of law or fact.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 143 allows the President to seek the Court's opinion.

questions.question: Statement 1: The power of contempt of court is inherent in the Supreme Court under Article 129. Statement 2: The Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, provides for the punishment of contempt.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 129 and the Contempt of Courts Act deal with contempt powers.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to transfer cases from one High Court to another under Article 139A. Statement 2: The Court can also transfer cases from a subordinate court to a High Court under the same article.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 139A provides for the transfer of cases between courts.

questions.question: Statement 1: The judiciary is independent as per the Constitution. Statement 2: The executive has no role in the appointment of judges.
questions.explanation: Only statement 1 is correct as the judiciary is independent but the executive has a role in appointments.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court of India has the power to review its own judgments under Article 137. Statement 2: The power of judicial review is implicit in Article 32 and Article 226 of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 137 gives the Supreme Court power to review its judgments and Articles 32 and 226 imply judicial review.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) established the doctrine of basic structure. Statement 2: The Golaknath case (1967) held that Parliament could not amend the Constitution to take away fundamental rights.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as they pertain to landmark cases affecting constitutional amendments and fundamental rights.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India appoints the Chief Justice of India and other judges of the Supreme Court. Statement 2: The appointment of judges is done in consultation with the Chief Justice and other senior judges.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the President appoints judges with consultation.

questions.question: Statement 1: Article 20 protects individuals from conviction for offenses not in force at the time of commission. Statement 2: Article 21 guarantees the right to life and personal liberty.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as they describe fundamental rights.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court can advise the President on legal matters under Article 143. Statement 2: The court's advisory jurisdiction is not binding on the President.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct regarding the court's advisory role.

questions.question: Statement 1: The 42nd Amendment Act (1976) curtailed the Supreme Court's power of judicial review. Statement 2: The 43rd and 44th Amendments partially restored judicial review powers.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct about the impact of amendments on judicial review.

questions.question: Statement 1: The concept of 'public interest litigation' (PIL) allows any person to approach the court on behalf of the public. Statement 2: PILs are filed under Articles 32 and 226 of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as PILs serve the public interest through these articles.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction in disputes between states or between a state and the Union. Statement 2: This jurisdiction is specified under Article 131 of the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct regarding the court's original jurisdiction.

questions.question: Statement 1: The 24th Amendment Act (1971) enabled Parliament to amend any part of the Constitution. Statement 2: The Kesavananda Bharati case later limited this power by introducing the basic structure doctrine.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct about the amendment and its limitation.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court can declare a law passed by Parliament or a state legislature void if it violates the Constitution. Statement 2: This power is a part of judicial review under the Indian Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct about the court's judicial review power.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the judiciary in India: 1. The Supreme Court has the power to review its own judgments under Article 137. 2. The Supreme Court can advise the President on matters of public importance under Article 143. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 137 grants the Supreme Court the power to review its judgments and Article 143 allows it to advise the President.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a consequence of the Supreme Court's landmark case of Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala (1973)? 1. The Parliament's power to amend the Constitution was restricted. 2. The doctrine of basic structure was established. Choose the correct option:
questions.explanation: The case established the doctrine of basic structure and restricted Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Indian judiciary: 1. The Chief Justice of India has the power to allocate cases to other judges. 2. The Supreme Court has original jurisdiction over disputes between states. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Chief Justice has administrative powers and the Supreme Court has original jurisdiction over interstate disputes under Article 131.

questions.question: Which of the following statements regarding the judiciary is/are correct? 1. The Supreme Court can declare a law passed by Parliament as unconstitutional under Article 13. 2. The Supreme Court has the power of judicial review. Choose the correct option:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court can declare laws as unconstitutional and has the power of judicial review.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the relationship between the judiciary and the executive in India: 1. The President appoints the Chief Justice of India. 2. The Supreme Court has the power to appoint its own judges. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Only the first statement is correct as the President appoints the Chief Justice.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a feature of the Indian judiciary? 1. Independence of the judiciary. 2. Separation of powers between the judiciary, legislature, and executive. Choose the correct option:
questions.explanation: Both independence of the judiciary and separation of powers are key features.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Supreme Court's power of judicial review: 1. It can review laws passed by Parliament. 2. It can review the decisions of lower courts. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court can review both laws and lower court decisions.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Indian judiciary is/are correct? 1. The Supreme Court is the highest court of appeal. 2. The Supreme Court has advisory jurisdiction. Choose the correct option:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court is the highest court of appeal and has advisory jurisdiction under Article 143.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the judiciary in India: 1. The judiciary is independent of the executive. 2. The judiciary can review the actions of the executive. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the judiciary is independent and can review executive actions.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a consequence of the 42nd Amendment to the Indian Constitution? 1. The power of the judiciary to review laws was restricted. 2. The word 'secular' was added to the preamble. Choose the correct option:
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment both restricted judicial review and added 'secular' to the preamble.

questions.question: Statement 1: The 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, limited the power of judicial review. Statement 2: The Supreme Court in the Minerva Mills case, 1980, struck down certain provisions of the 42nd Amendment.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the 42nd Amendment did limit judicial review, which was later challenged in the Minerva Mills case.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Chief Justice of India has the power to allocate cases under Article 145. Statement 2: The Supreme Court in the S.P. Gupta case, 1981, established the principle of public interest litigation.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Chief Justice does have the power to allocate cases and the S.P. Gupta case did establish public interest litigation.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for a single integrated judiciary. Statement 2: The High Courts have the power of superintendence over subordinate courts under Article 227.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as India has a single integrated judiciary and High Courts do have superintendence power.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case, 1973, established the doctrine of basic structure. Statement 2: The 24th Amendment Act, 1971, was struck down by the Supreme Court for violating the basic structure.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Kesavananda Bharati case did establish the basic structure doctrine and the 24th Amendment was challenged on these grounds.

questions.question: Statement 1: The President of India appoints the Chief Justice and other judges of the Supreme Court. Statement 2: The Second Judges case, 1993, established the collegium system for appointment of judges.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the President does appoint judges and the Second Judges case did establish the collegium system.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to advise the President under Article 143. Statement 2: The advisory jurisdiction of the Supreme Court is not binding on the President.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Supreme Court does have advisory power and its advice is not binding.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Constitution provides for the independence of the judiciary. Statement 2: The independence of the judiciary is a basic feature of the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Constitution does ensure judicial independence and it is a basic feature.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court in the Golaknath case, 1967, held that Parliament could not amend fundamental rights. Statement 2: The 25th Amendment Act, 1971, was passed to overcome the Golaknath judgment.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Golaknath case did limit Parliament's power and the 25th Amendment was a response.

questions.question: Statement 1: The High Courts have the power of judicial review under Article 226. Statement 2: The Supreme Court in the L. Chandra Kumar case, 1997, held that the power of judicial review is inherent in the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as High Courts do have judicial review power and it is an inherent constitutional power.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for the independence of the judiciary. Statement 2: The Chief Justice of India is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the Prime Minister.
questions.explanation: Only statement 2 is incorrect as the Chief Justice is appointed by the President on the recommendation of the outgoing Chief Justice.

questions.question: Statement 1: The doctrine of 'basic structure' was introduced by the Supreme Court in the Kesavananda Bharati case. Statement 2: The 42nd Amendment Act, 1976, gave unparalleled power to the Parliament to amend the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, the basic structure doctrine was established in Kesavananda Bharati and the 42nd Amendment enhanced Parliament's power.

questions.question: Statement 1: The power of the Supreme Court to adjudicate disputes between the Centre and States is provided under Article 131. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has advisory jurisdiction under Article 143.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, Article 131 and 143 provide for the Supreme Court's original and advisory jurisdiction respectively.

questions.question: Statement 1: The concept of 'public interest litigation' was introduced in the case of SP Gupta vs Union of India. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has the power to initiate suo motu proceedings in matters of public interest.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, SP Gupta case introduced PIL and the Supreme Court can initiate suo motu proceedings.

questions.question: Statement 1: The appointment of judges to the Supreme Court is made by the President on the recommendation of the Collegium. Statement 2: The Second Judges Case established the primacy of the Collegium in the appointment of judges.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, the Collegium recommends appointments and the Second Judges Case solidified its role.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Indian Constitution provides for a single integrated judiciary. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has the power to transfer cases from one High Court to another under Article 139A.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, India has a single integrated judiciary and the Supreme Court can transfer cases between High Courts.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court can review the decisions of administrative tribunals under Article 136. Statement 2: The Administrative Tribunals Act, 1985, provides for the establishment of tribunals for adjudicating disputes related to public services.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, the Supreme Court can review tribunal decisions under Article 136 and the Act establishes administrative tribunals.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Constitution (99th Amendment) Act, 2014, established the National Judicial Appointments Commission. Statement 2: The Supreme Court struck down the 99th Amendment as unconstitutional in the Fourth Judges Case.
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct, the 99th Amendment established the NJAC and the Supreme Court later struck it down.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the judiciary: 1. The Supreme Court of India has the power to review its own judgments under Article 137. 2. The power of judicial review is implicit in Article 32 and Article 226 of the Constitution. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as Article 137 gives the Supreme Court the power to review its judgments and the power of judicial review is implicit in Articles 32 and 226.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the judiciary and the executive: 1. The President of India has the power to appoint judges to the Supreme Court on the recommendation of the Chief Justice of India. 2. The Chief Justice of India and other judges of the Supreme Court can be removed by a two-thirds majority of the Lok Sabha. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct as the President appoints judges on the recommendation of the Chief Justice, but statement 2 is incorrect as removal requires a two-thirds majority of both Houses of Parliament.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are a consequence of the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973)?
options: Introduction of the Basic Structure doctrine
Limitation on Parliament's power to amend the Constitution
questions.explanation: The Kesavananda Bharati case introduced the Basic Structure doctrine, limiting Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the independence of the judiciary: 1. The salaries and allowances of judges are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India. 2. The conduct of judges can be discussed in Parliament only on a motion for removal. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as judges' salaries are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India and their conduct can be discussed in Parliament only on a motion for removal.

questions.question: What is the primary consequence of the Second Judges Case (1993)?
questions.explanation: The Second Judges Case introduced the collegium system for appointing judges.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Supreme Court's power of judicial review: 1. The Supreme Court can review laws made by Parliament and state legislatures. 2. The Supreme Court can review administrative actions of the executive. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the Supreme Court can review laws and administrative actions.

questions.question: Which article of the Constitution deals with the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Article 124 deals with the appointment of judges to the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the relationship between the judiciary and the legislature: 1. The judiciary has the power to declare laws made by the legislature as unconstitutional. 2. The legislature has the power to impeach judges. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both statements are correct as the judiciary can declare laws unconstitutional and the legislature can impeach judges.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Golaknath case (1967)?
options: Introduction of the doctrine of prospective overruling
Limitation on Parliament's power to amend the Constitution
questions.explanation: The Golaknath case limited Parliament's power to amend the Constitution.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the powers of the Chief Justice of India: 1. The Chief Justice has the power to allocate cases to other judges. 2. The Chief Justice has the power to appoint judges to the Supreme Court. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1 is correct as the Chief Justice allocates cases, but statement 2 is incorrect as the Chief Justice recommends appointments.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court of India has the power to review its own judgments under Article 137. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also review the judgments of the High Courts under Article 137. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Article 137 gives the Supreme Court the power to review its own judgments.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Chief Justice of India has the power to appoint judges to the Supreme Court. Statement 2: The President of India appoints judges to the Supreme Court on the recommendation of the Chief Justice of India. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The President appoints judges on the recommendation of the Chief Justice and other senior judges.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Kesavananda Bharati case (1973) is a landmark judgment that established the doctrine of basic structure. Statement 2: The Minerva Mills case (1980) further reinforced the doctrine of basic structure. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Both cases are landmark judgments that established and reinforced the doctrine of basic structure.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court has the power to declare a law passed by Parliament as unconstitutional under Article 13. Statement 2: The Supreme Court can also declare a law passed by a state legislature as unconstitutional under Article 13. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court can declare laws passed by Parliament or state legislatures as unconstitutional under Article 13.

questions.question: Statement 1: The Supreme Court of India has original jurisdiction in cases involving disputes between states. Statement 2: The Supreme Court has appellate jurisdiction in cases involving disputes between states and the Centre. Which of the statements is/are correct?
